ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301710 'fruit juice drink' is the closest available class; no specific 'mixed berries juice drink' class exists in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code AN0KN 'Fruit nectars and drinks' is the closest defensible match; no berry-specific juice drink subcode exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for mixed berries juice drink as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving is one glass of approximately 250 ml, equivalent to 250 g, providing about 108 kcal and 23.6 g of sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Mixed Berries Juice Drink — Singapore HPB Foodo Database (D03070021)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo record D03070021 for Mixed Berries Juice Drink. Saturated fat not reported by HPB; set to 0.0 as consistent with a low-fat juice drink formulation. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and other micronutrients not reported by HPB; set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 'mixed berries juice drink' in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 52 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: apple juice (unsweetened, GI ~41–44) and fruit juice drinks with added sugar (GI ~47–55 range per Atkinson et al. 2008 and Foster-Powell et al. 2002). Given the high sugar content (9.45 g/100g, predominantly fructose and sucrose from berry concentrate and added sweeteners), a GI of 52 is a defensible mid-range estimate for a sweetened berry juice drink. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (52 × 23.6 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 12.3, rounded to 12.2.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (9.45 g/100g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently raises blood pressure risk via insulin-mediated sodium retention and weight gain, despite low sodium (8 mg/100g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this sugar-sweetened beverage, as regular SSB consumption independently elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines regardless of its low sodium content; replace with plain water or unsweetened berry-infused water.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 9.45 g sugar/100g and negligible dietary fibre (0.1 g/100g); liquid sugar is rapidly absorbed, causing significant postprandial glucose excursions.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid this beverage entirely, as it qualifies as a sugar-sweetened beverage under ADA guidelines and its liquid sugar matrix causes rapid glycaemic spikes; substitute with plain water, unsweetened sparkling water, or whole fresh berries.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased triglycerides, reduced HDL cholesterol, and elevated cardiovascular risk independent of total caloric intake.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit or eliminate this sugar-sweetened beverage, as habitual SSB intake is linked to dyslipidaemia and increased CVD risk per AHA dietary guidelines; choose unsweetened beverages instead.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (9.45 g/100g) in a processed juice drink promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; negligible fibre and absence of whole-food polyphenols negate any potential anti-inflammatory benefit from berry-derived ingredients.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this drink and choose whole fresh berries instead, which retain fibre and intact polyphenols without the pro-inflammatory effect of added sugar.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mixed Berries Juice Drink is a commercially produced, ultra-processed sweetened beverage made from a blend of various berry juice concentrates (such as strawberry, blueberry, raspberry, and blackcurrant), water, and added sweeteners. It is not a pure fruit juice; it is a juice drink, meaning it contains a lower proportion of actual fruit juice topped up with water and sugar or other sweeteners to achieve a palatable flavour. The drink has a light, sweet, fruity taste with mild berry tartness and a thin, watery texture typical of diluted juice drinks. It is typically served chilled and consumed as a refreshment or snack beverage. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 43 kcal, 0.1 g protein, 0.06 g fat, 10.34 g carbohydrate, and 9.45 g sugar, with negligible dietary fibre (0.1 g) and low sodium (8 mg). The high sugar-to-fibre ratio classifies it as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB). Clinically, it is unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes due to rapid glucose absorption from liquid sugar, and it poses moderate concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease through SSB-mediated metabolic pathways. It is low in micronutrients and provides no meaningful protein, fat, or fibre benefit. It is suitable for vegetarians, vegans, and those following halal or gluten-free diets.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database record D03070021 with confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008, documented as such. All values are internally consistent and plausible for a sweetened berry juice drink.
notes: Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this mixed berries juice drink are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database record D03070021 as of 2026. Calcium_mg and iron_mg are set to 0.0 as confirmed zero per HPB source record. Saturated_fat_g is set to 0.0 consistent with the negligible total fat (0.06 g/100g) reported by HPB; a juice drink of this composition would contain no meaningful saturated fat. Protein_g (0.1 g) and fat_g (0.06 g) are near-trace values confirmed by HPB; they are not zero and no zero-macro justification is required. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database fruit juice drink entries and Atkinson et al. 2008; no direct GI measurement for this specific product was found. Dietary tags 'low_sodium' and 'low_calorie' from input are retained as contextually accurate (sodium 8 mg/100g; energy 43 kcal/100g) but the beverage is not recommended for metabolic health conditions due to its SSB classification.
